findings.summary: FakeGeminiClient in test_image_generation.py is a hand-rolled stub that subclasses the real client without calling super().__init__, bypassing all initialization and returning hardcoded fake data. This violates the project's stated zero-mock policy and means the test exercises only delegation shape, not actual behavior.
evidence: test_image_generation.py lines 17-32: class FakeGeminiClient(GeminiClient): def __init__(self, config=None): self.calls = [] — no super().__init__, returns hardcoded b'fake_data'
ImageGenerator.generate() is a one-line pass-through to self.client.generate_images(); the fake test only confirms delegation but cannot catch any real failure in image generation logic

CLAUDE.md Zero-Mock Policy states: 'Tests never use unittest.mock, MagicMock, monkeypatch, or pytest-mock. External dependencies use @pytest.mark.skipif guards.' — a hand-rolled Fake class with hardcoded return values is semantically a stub and breaches the spirit of this policy
The real @_skip_no_key path (line 50) is correct zero-mock practice; but the fake path provides false assurance without testing any production path
findings.suggestion: Replace FakeGeminiClient stub with a real GeminiClient under @pytest.mark.skipif(not GEMINI_API_KEY) guard.

findings.summary: A large fraction of unit tests across the codebase use docstrings of the form 'Test functionality: <thing>.' These add no specification value — they simply restate the test method name. This pattern appears in at least 3,978 occurrences across 205 test files, indicating they were mass-generated rather than authored with intent.
evidence: grep across all test files finds 3,978 occurrences of the literal string '"""Test functionality:' across 205 files
test_trust_gateway.py line 42: def test_untrusted_value(self): '"""Test functionality: untrusted value."""' — the docstring contributes nothing beyond the method name
test_mcp_bridge.py line 42: def test_returns_mcp_server(self): '"""Test functionality: returns mcp server."""' — same restating pattern
Docstrings like these provide no specification of expected behavior, edge cases, or why the assertion matters; they will never fail if the assertion is wrong
findings.suggestion: Replace Test functionality: X. docstrings with outcome-oriented descriptions of what the test verifies.

findings.summary: The 87 auto-discovered mcp_tools.py modules each expose tools that go through the discovery->registry->trust-gateway->execution path. Unit tests cover the registry and gateway in isolation, but there are no integration tests that exercise the end-to-end call path for the most-used tool categories (git_operations, data_visualization, security). The handful of integration tests that exist target cross-module orchestration or visualization performance, not the MCP dispatch path itself.
evidence: git_operations/mcp_tools.py has 35 tools; the integration test directory has no file for git_operations MCP end-to-end execution
test_mcp_bridge.py tests server creation and registry listing, but trusted_call_tool() with real tool invocation is not exercised across the mcp_tools.py surface

Only 3 integration test files found targeting MCP flows: test_cross_module_workflows.py (3 uses of 'Test functionality:' docstrings — also bulk-generated), calendar_integration/test_mcp_tools.py, and test_visualization_integration.py
The trust_gateway.trusted_call_tool() confirmation flow (lines 710-751 of trust_gateway.py) has zero test coverage visible in the integration test suite
findings.suggestion: Replace Test functionality: X. docstrings with outcome-oriented descriptions of what the test verifies.

findings.summary: MCP tool functions across the codebase return errors in two incompatible shapes: {"status": "error", "message": ...} and {"error": ...}. The git_operations module uses the status/message shape consistently (35 occurrences). The proxy_tools.py static tools use {"error": ...} directly (tool_module_info, tool_list_module_functions, tool_call_module_function). Callers that check for one shape will silently pass through errors from the other shape.
evidence: proxy_tools.py line 36: return {"error": f"Module not found: {module_name}", "detail": str(exc)} — uses bare 'error' key
proxy_tools.py line 128: return {"error": f"Function {func_name!r} not found in {module_path}.", "available": available[:30]} — also bare 'error' key
git_operations/mcp_tools.py lines 29-30: return {"status": "error", "message": str(exc)} — uses status/message shape on all 35 error returns
git_analysis/mcp_tools.py uses {"status": "error", "message": ...} throughout (23 occurrences), as do data_visualization, security, and most other mcp_tools.py files

The two shapes are not interchangeable: a caller checking result.get('status') == 'error' will silently ignore failures from proxy_tools.py; a caller checking result.get('error') will silently ignore failures from git_operations tools
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that exercises trusted_call_tool end-to-end with a real tool call.

findings.summary: The pattern 'except Exception as exc: return {"status": "error", "message": str(exc)}' or 'except Exception as e: return {"error": ...}' appears 186+ times across 50 mcp_tools.py files. While MCP tools should not propagate raw exceptions to callers, catching bare Exception at every tool boundary means programming errors, import failures, and unexpected state all produce the same error shape, making root-cause diagnosis impossible without tracing and losing exception type information.
evidence: git_operations/mcp_tools.py has 35 occurrences of 'except Exception as exc: return {"status": "error", ...}' — one on every single tool function

logging_monitoring/mcp_tools.py line 51: except Exception as exc: return {"status": "error", "message": str(exc)} — catches everything on a formatting utility that can only fail for legitimate domain reasons (bad level string, import error)
git_analysis/mcp_tools.py has 16 occurrences — both GitNexus (subprocess failure, expected) and GitPython (pure Python, unexpected) tools use the same broad catch
str(exc) loses exception type; for example, a TypeError from wrong argument count looks identical to an OSError from a missing file
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that exercises trusted_call_tool end-to-end with a real tool call.

findings.summary: trust_gateway.py implements SAFE_TOOLS, SAFE_TOOL_COUNT, and DESTRUCTIVE_TOOL_COUNT as module-level constants via a __getattr__ override (lines 296-311) that computes and caches them into globals() on first access. This is a non-idiomatic Python pattern that makes the module's public surface invisible to static analysis, IDE completion, and import-time introspection. The caching via globals() is fragile — a subsequent invalidate_tool_cache() call will leave the cached globals stale.
evidence: trust_gateway.py lines 296-311: def __getattr__(name: str): if name == 'SAFE_TOOLS': val = _get_safe_tools(); globals()['SAFE_TOOLS'] = val; return val
The docstring on __getattr__ says 'cached on first access' but there is no cache invalidation path — if discover_dynamic_tools() adds new tools after first access, SAFE_TOOLS stays stale

test_trust_gateway.py imports SAFE_TOOL_COUNT, DESTRUCTIVE_TOOL_COUNT at module level (line 15-16), which forces the __getattr__ cache to populate at import time; any later tool discovery changes are invisible to subsequent test assertions

A simpler approach would be module-level functions (get_safe_tools(), get_destructive_tool_count()) which are honest about being dynamic; or true lazy_property using a wrapper class — not globals() mutation inside __getattr__
findings.suggestion: Replace __getattr__ globals() mutation with explicit module-level constants or a lazy accessor function.

findings.summary: tool_call_module_function in proxy_tools.py (lines 130-139) has a try-except nested inside another try-except to attempt JSON serialization of the result. The inner try is used for control flow (testing serializability) rather than error recovery. This makes the function's error paths confusing: an outer Exception returns an 'error' dict, but an inner TypeError/ValueError silently converts the result to a string without surfacing the conversion.
evidence: proxy_tools.py lines 130-139: try: result = func(**kwargs); try: _json.dumps(result); except (TypeError, ValueError): result = str(result); return {'result': result}; except Exception as e: return {'error': ...}

The inner try/except catches serialization failure and silently stringifies without any warning or log — a caller cannot distinguish 'the function returned a string' from 'the function returned something unserializable that was coerced'
The import of json as _json inside the hot path of the function (line 133) also re-imports on every call, though Python module import caching means this is a style issue rather than a performance one
findings.suggestion: Standardize all MCP tool error returns to the same shape: either {error: msg} or {status: error, message: msg}.

findings.summary: Several directories show camelCase identifiers in Python code — a clear violation of PEP 8 in a codebase that is otherwise uniformly snake_case. The blind packet identifies camelCase counts in: performance/ (5), transport/ (9), utils/ (4), model_context_protocol/ (1), git_operations/ (1), terminal_interface/ (1), lora/ (5), website/ (3), shells/ (1).
evidence: Blind packet naming_by_directory shows: performance/ has 5 camelCase identifiers, transport/ has 9, utils/ has 4, lora/ has 5, website/ has 3 — all in directories that are otherwise 100% snake_case
These are likely parameter names, local variable names, or method names that leaked from JavaScript-style code generation
The rest of the codebase (130+ directories) shows zero or trivially low camelCase counts, making these islands clearly outlier style drift
findings.suggestion: Rename camelCase variables to snake_case; JSON dict keys using MCP protocol names are exempt.

findings.summary: 3,978 test method docstrings across 205 files use the template 'Test functionality: <noun phrase>.' that exactly restates the method name without adding any specification, precondition, or rationale. This is a hallmark LLM generation artifact — docstring bloat on trivial 1-3 line test functions that assert a single value.
evidence: test_trust_gateway.py line 42: def test_untrusted_value(self): '"""Test functionality: untrusted value."""' assert TrustLevel.UNTRUSTED.value == 'untrusted' — 3 lines total, docstring adds zero information
test_trust_gateway.py line 46: def test_verified_value(self): '"""Test functionality: verified value."""' — same restating pattern for every enum value test
The pattern appears verbatim in 205 files and 3,978 places — the uniformity is not achievable by human authorship; it is a template artifact
Legitimate test docstrings explain WHY the behavior is expected, edge cases, or references to spec/ticket; none of these 3,978 instances does any of that
findings.suggestion: Replace Test functionality: X. docstrings with outcome-oriented descriptions of what the test verifies.

findings.summary: The __getattr__ override in trust_gateway.py (lines 296-311) uses globals() mutation to implement a caching pattern for three module-level constants. This is defensive over-engineering: the constants are needed only because test code imports them by name at module level. The actual callers at runtime always call the underlying functions directly. The pattern adds indirection without adding safety.
evidence: trust_gateway.py lines 296-311 implement __getattr__ as a lazy cache for SAFE_TOOLS, SAFE_TOOL_COUNT, DESTRUCTIVE_TOOL_COUNT

The comment on line 296 says 'Lazy module-level accessors — computed on first access and cached.' — the caching purpose is to avoid import-time computation, but the functions _get_safe_tools() and _get_destructive_tools() are already cheap (they iterate a dict)

The pattern was likely generated to handle a specific import ordering problem (tests importing the constants before discovery runs) — the correct fix would be to make the test assertions call the functions rather than cache constants in globals()
Using globals()['SAFE_TOOLS'] = val inside __getattr__ is an advanced Python pattern that most readers will not expect to find in a production module; it creates invisible mutable module state
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that exercises trusted_call_tool end-to-end with a real tool call.

findings.summary: Every one of the 87 mcp_tools.py files uses structurally identical try/except Exception blocks with the same return dict shape. The boilerplate is so uniform (186+ occurrences across 50 files, 223+ 'status: error, message' occurrences) that it was clearly generated as a template rather than designed per-tool. This means no tool has differentiated error handling — a missing dependency, a bad argument, and an infrastructure failure all look identical to callers.
evidence: 186 occurrences of 'except Exception' across 50 mcp_tools.py files — every file, every function, every error path uses the same pattern
223 occurrences of 'return {"status": "error", "message"' across 49 files — the exact same dict structure on every error return
git_operations/mcp_tools.py alone has 35 consecutive tool functions each with: try: ... return {'status': 'success', ...} except Exception as exc: return {'status': 'error', 'message': str(exc)}
No tool in any mcp_tools.py file uses a narrower exception type (ValueError, OSError, ImportError) — all catch base Exception, which is a code-generation artifact, not a deliberate design choice
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that exercises trusted_call_tool end-to-end with a real tool call.
